results.possibleIdentity: Robert Knisel was a professional printer and artist collaborator active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily known for his work at the renowned print studio Gemini G.E.L. (Graphic Editions Limited) in Los Angeles. Working under master printer Kenneth Tyler, Knisel specialized in screenprinting and lithography, assisting some of the most prominent artists of the era in projects that often explored the intersection of art and technology. His notable collaborations include assisting Keith Sonnier on the "Video Still Screen" series (1973), which focused on television static and information systems, and working with Andy Warhol on the "Vote McGovern" (1972) campaign poster. He is also credited on editions by Frank Stella (including the "Purple Series" and works like "Fūrg (State II)"), Sam Francis (e.g., "Freshet" and "Red Again"), Robert Motherwell ("Summer Light Series"), Ronald Davis, and Douglas Huebler. His role as a technical expert facilitating artist visions aligns with the collaborative spirit of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted partnerships between artists and technical specialists.

Robert A. Knisel (1920–2010) was an artist and textile executive who, after a 37-year career in the textile industry at J.P. Stevens in New York City, became a prominent figure in the Florida arts community. He served as the president of the Panama Art Association and played a key role in the development of the Visual Arts Center (VAC) in Panama City, where he also taught oil painting. While his professional career was in textiles in New York, he was an active painter and arts administrator in his later years. It is possible this identity overlaps with the technical expertise required for high-end art production, although the 'Printer' identity at Gemini G.E.L. in Los Angeles is more directly linked to the specific avant-garde and technological art movements of the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Kent Holloway (1935–2004) was a San Francisco-based artist known for his work in painting and experimental light-based media. He was active in the San Francisco Renaissance and Beat scene during the late 1950s and 1960s, often appearing in the social and artistic circles of figures like poet ruth weiss and artist Sutter Marin. During this period, his work was described as a form of symbolist painting characterized by a search for ancestral motifs. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, Holloway became an early collaborator at the Exploratorium in San Francisco. Working alongside artist Bob Miller, he developed light and optics exhibits that used the sun's rays to 'paint the air' with refracted colors. This work was associated with a local group interested in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. His catalog of work includes oil paintings such as 'Coney Island' (1966) and 'Portrait of Edgar (Once Removed)' (1961), as well as numerous nudes and studies from the late 1950s.

results.possibleIdentity: Jack Ward is a modernist sculptor active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is most noted for creating a large, black steel abstract sculpture in Gordon Park, Detroit, in 1977. While the piece was originally conceived as a modernist work to adorn the neighborhood, it has since become an iconic monument in the collective memory of Detroit, commemorating the civil rights riots that began in 1967. His work is characterized by modernist language and the use of industrial materials like steel.

Jack Ward is an electronic engineer and technology historian who has been active in the electronics industry since the late 1960s. Born in 1949, he is the founder and director of the Transistor Museum, a project dedicated to preserving and documenting the history of early semiconductor technology. His career involves bridging the gap between technical engineering and historical preservation, conducting numerous oral histories with pioneering scientists and engineers. His work aligns with the mission of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) which emphasize the intersection of technical expertise and creative documentation.

Jack Ward is a photographer who was active in the New York art and advertising scene during the 1960s and 1970s. He served as an apprentice and primary assistant to the renowned photographer Art Kane from 1960 to 1965, working out of Kane's Carnegie Hall studio. Ward was deeply involved in the production of influential editorial and advertising imagery, most notably the historic Marlboro cigarette campaigns. His work focused on Western landscapes and the portrayal of 'real' cowboys, moving away from stylized studio photography toward a more authentic visual narrative.

results.possibleIdentity: Lotte Cherin is a Southern California-based artist, technologist, and arts administrator who has been active in the intersection of art and technology since the late 1960s. Professionally, she served for over two decades as a Senior Client Technologies Analyst and User Consultant at Pepperdine University's Graduate School of Education and Psychology (GSEP). In the arts, she is a metalsmith and sculptor whose work was featured in the 1995 exhibition 'ALCHEMY: Metals Made Precious' at Cabrillo College. She has a historical connection to electronic music pioneer Ruth White, appearing in credits associated with White's labels, Rhythms Productions and Tom Thumb Records, which specialized in educational recordings and early Moog synthesizer compositions in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Cherin was also involved in the Los Angeles feminist art movement, with her name appearing in the archival records of the Woman's Building for the period 1980–1985. More recently, she has served as the Chair of the Malibu Arts Commission, where she has been a prominent advocate for public art and the establishment of a Malibu Arts Center.

results.possibleIdentity: Lewis MacAdams (1944–2020) was a prominent American poet, journalist, filmmaker, and environmental activist who described his activism as a form of performance art. Active in the late 1960s and 1970s, he was a member of the New York School of poets and later a key figure in the Bolinas, California, poetry community. In 1985, he co-founded the Friends of the Los Angeles River (FoLAR), which he famously characterized as a "40-year artwork" and a long-term performance piece intended to restore the river to its natural state. His work frequently bridged the gap between poetry, performance, and political lobbying, involving collaborations with artists, community members, and governmental engineers. MacAdams served as the director of the Poetry Center at San Francisco State University from 1975 to 1978 and authored over a dozen books of poetry, including "The River" (2005) and "Water Charms" (1968). His interest in interdisciplinary collaboration and the intersection of art and institutional systems aligns with the ethos of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); notably, his work is included in the National Gallery of Art collections alongside other avant-garde artists and portfolios from the 1970s, such as "Fits No. 2" (1972).

results.possibleIdentity: Clark Coolidge (born February 26, 1939) is a prominent American experimental poet and jazz drummer who has been active since the early 1960s. He is widely recognized for his associations with the Language School and the New York School of poets. His work is characterized by a unique exploration of language as a material, often incorporating geological and scientific motifs derived from his studies in geology at Brown University. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Coolidge was deeply embedded in the avant-garde scene of New York and San Francisco. He participated in multidisciplinary projects that bridged the gap between literature, music, and the visual arts. He was a frequent contributor to the experimental magazine '0 to 9' (1967–1969), edited by Vito Acconci and Bernadette Mayer. Coolidge is also known for his musical career, having played drums for the 1960s psychedelic rock band Serpent Power. His connection to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is evidenced by his participation in the collaborative 'Poetry Event' held at Robert Rauschenberg's loft in the late 1960s and his involvement in improvised music projects that integrated new technologies and recorded sounds. Throughout his career, he has published over fifty books, including 'Flag Flutter & U.S. Electric' (1966), 'Space' (1970), and 'The Crystal Text' (1986).
